# NFR Profile — Landing Site, Multi-Provider Auth, and Billing

**Feature:** 2026-07-01-landing-auth-billing
**Date:** 2026-07-01
**Generated by:** /definition skill

---

## Performance Targets

- **Landing page response time**: No explicit SLA defined. Target: render within normal Fly.io Node.js response time (< 200ms server-side; no client-side JS rendering required).
- **Credit balance check latency**: The `credits` guard adds one Postgres query per turn. No explicit SLA. Acceptable overhead in MVP given Neon serverless connection reuse.
- **Stripe Checkout redirect**: No explicit SLA. Stripe API response time is outside platform control.
- **PostHog event capture**: Non-blocking fire-and-forget pattern. Must not delay landing page, auth callback, or turn response.

## Security Requirements

- **`req.session.accessToken` is the canonical session token field** — never `req.session.token`. Enforced across all auth handlers and `authGuard`.
- **`rotateSessionId` is mandatory after every provider login** — prevents session fixation. Applies to GitHub, Google, and email/password providers.
- **`accessToken` is never written to disk, Redis, or any HTML response** — `_sanitiseForRedis` strips it before Redis writes. HTML templates must not reference session token values.
- **Passwords are never stored in plaintext, never logged** — bcrypt hash with cost factor ≥ 10. Enforced in lab-s2.2 (AC5).
- **CSRF state parameter is mandatory on every OAuth callback** — applies to GitHub (existing) and Google (lab-s2.1 AC7). State mismatch → 403.
- **Stripe webhook signature verification is non-bypassable in production** — raw body required; JSON body parser must not run on `/webhook/stripe`.
- **Path traversal guard**: No file writes occur from user-supplied paths in this feature — guard applies to the existing disk write patterns but no new ones are introduced here.
- **Rate limiting on email/password auth endpoints** — 10 attempts / 5 minutes per IP (lab-s2.2 AC4).
- **Brute force protection on Stripe webhook endpoint** — Stripe signature verification is the primary protection; no additional rate limiting needed on `/webhook/stripe` in MVP.

## Data Classification

**Public**: Landing page HTML (no user data)
**Internal**: Credit balance per tenant, Stripe customer ID, `stripe_event_id` idempotency records, `firstLogin` flag
**Confidential**: `req.session.accessToken` (GitHub/Google OAuth tokens), bcrypt password hashes, `DATABASE_URL`, `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`, `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
**Restricted**: Raw Stripe webhook payloads (contain payment event data — handled by Stripe, not stored by platform)

## Data Residency

Not applicable — Neon Postgres is US-based (existing provisioning); no data residency requirements defined for this feature.

## Availability SLA

Not defined for MVP. Platform is on Fly.io free/hobby tier. Expected uptime matches Fly.io baseline.

## Compliance Frameworks

None. Stripe handles cardholder data — the platform is out of PCI DSS scope (Stripe Checkout, not custom payment form). No GDPR, HIPAA, SOX, or AML/CFT obligations identified.

## No-External-Credentials Constraint

All secrets (`STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`, `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`, `DATABASE_URL`) are Fly.io secrets. None appear in committed code, `.env` files, or log output.

---

**Status**: Active — reviewed at definition 2026-07-01. No NFRs with named regulatory clauses — no human sign-off required beyond operator (Hamish King) self-review.
